model_narrative: We blend several feeds into a single feed mix. Each feed has a known price per ton and contributes known nutrient levels to the mix. We decide what fraction of the mix comes from each feed. The objective is to make the total cost of the mix per ton as small as possible.

sets.name: f
sets.doc: the feeds available to blend into the mix

sets.name: n
sets.doc: the nutrients whose levels in the mix are regulated

params.name: price
params.doc: price of each feed, in fgld per ton

params.name: req
params.doc: minimum required level of each nutrient in the mix, as a percent

params.name: char_mean
params.doc: mean level of a nutrient contributed by a feed, as a percent

params.name: char_variance
params.doc: variance of the nutrient level contributed by a feed, as a percent; provided for protein only and not used by any linear requirement

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: total cost of the mix per ton, in fgld

vars.name: x
vars.doc: fraction of each feed in the mix, expressed as a share of the whole mix

constraints.name: cost
constraints.intent: The total cost of the mix is the price of each feed multiplied by the fraction of the mix that comes from that feed, summed over every feed. Set the cost variable equal to that total.

constraints.name: x[f]
constraints.intent: The fractions of the feeds must form a complete mix. The fractions taken across all feeds must add up to one.

constraints.name: char_mean[n,
constraints.intent: Each nutrient must reach its required level in the mix. For every nutrient, the level it gets from each feed weighted by that feed's fraction, summed over all feeds, must be at least the required level for that nutrient.

query: Both of the protein-dense oilseed feeds — sesame and ground meal — track the same volatile export market, so procurement is floating a combined ceiling: together they shouldn't exceed a quarter of the blend. We're looking at that scenario. What does the optimizer return for the feed cost?

query: There's a proposal on the table to keep our highest-protein feed in serious rotation: require ground meal to carry at least as much of the blend as oats does, so the premium meal supplier always beats the oats contract on volume. Plug that in and tell me where the feed cost lands.

query: Engineering is pitching a two-part resilience package and wants it evaluated together: first, stop leaning so hard on barley by holding it under 40% of the blend; and second, build a 5% safety margin into the protein spec so the delivered protein comes in at least five percent above the breed requirement. Run that combined scenario and walk me through the resulting feed cost.
expected_pyomo:
model.cap_barley = Constraint(expr=model.x['barley'] <= 0.40)
model.protein_buffer = Constraint(expr=sum(model.char_mean['protein', f] * model.x[f] for f in model.f) >= 1.05 * model.req['protein'])

query: Barley's dominating the mix at almost 70%. Couldn't we require no single feed make up more than 50% of the blend? What's keeping the model from spreading the buy out?

query: The cheap end of the price list (barley + oats) is carrying almost 70% of the mix. Shouldn't we cap that pair combined at 60% so we're not over-leaning on the cheap side? What's keeping the model from doing that?

query: Every batch lands right on the bare minimum for both protein and fat — zero margin, and the vet has flagged that the spec drifts between deliveries. Shouldn't each nutrient come in at least ten percent above its requirement so we're not one bad mix away from a violation? What's keeping the model from carrying that buffer on its own?

query: Half the feed list barely shows up in the plan. Shouldn't every feed pull at least 10% of the blend so we keep all four suppliers in rotation? What's keeping the model from balancing it out?
